1: <header>Multilevel life cycle assessment to evaluate prospective agricultural development scenarios in a semi-arid irrigated region of Tunisia </header>

L. Pradeleix a, , P. Roux b , S. Bouarfa a , V. Bellon-Maurel b a G-EAU, AgroParisTech, Cirad, IRD, INRAE, Montpellier SupAgro, Univ Montpellier, ELSA Research Group, Montpellier, France b ITAP, INRAE, L'Institut Agro-Montpellier SupAgro, Univ Montpellier, ELSA Research Group and ELSA-PACT Industrial Chair, Montpellier, France
2:
<list>Multilevel Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) was conducted in a semi-arid irrigated plain (Tunisia). 
Agrarian System Diagnosis and Life Cycle Assessment methods were coupled. 
Environmental hotspots are modeled from the plot /herd, up to the regional scale. 
Main impacts are caused by water depletion, fertilizer manufacturing and manure transportation. 
Environmental impacts may increase by 40% in trend evolution scenarios (watertable depletion and less familial farming).</list>

0: Keywords: Water-food-energy nexus Global change Climate change Irrigation Agrarian system diagnosis Land planning
1: CONTEXT: To design policies for sustainable irrigation planning, public decision-makers need knowledge about the potential environmental impacts of irrigated farming systems. In a previous paper, we proposed to couple Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) and Agrarian System Diagnosis (ASD) to build the Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) of a farming region in a context of data scarcity and farming system diversity. This method was applied to the semi-arid irrigated plain of Kairouan (Tunisia).

OBJECTIVES: The aim of the present paper is to demonstrate how this new ASD-based LCA method is used to conduct the impact assessment of this farming region, under current conditions and for two prospective scenarios. METHODS: Two functional units (FU) were used: the area and the gross product generated. Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) was conducted based on a multilevel process, by converting LCI data collected for each cropping/ livestock system into LCIA outputs, which were then consolidated at the farming system (FS) and at the regional scales. Two scenarios, based on observed trends, were assessed: the "agrarian" scenario (driven by a shift from family to corporate farming) and the "hydrological" scenario (driven by a water table drawdown). RESULTS: Environmental hotspots were identified at each scale: the most impactful FS was the "mixed family farming" (intermediate access to water), because of its large share of land at the regional scale (45%). In this FS, the most harmful cropping system was input-intensive olive groves intercropped with a melon-pepper/ cereal/ tomato rotation, in which most impacts (35%) were attributable to the input-intensive "melon" crop, and more particularly to "manufacture and transport of fertilizer", including farmyard manure. Globally, the most harmful

processes were water and fertilizer use (fertilizer manufacturing and transportation). This downscaling is very useful to inform public policies on the most harmful productions. The impacts in human, ecosystem and resource endpoints increased by 11%, 2%, 9% and 23%, 27% and 40%, for the agrarian and hydrological scenarios respectively.

SIGNIFICANCE: These results show that observed trends in farming practices evolution (more irrigation) should be stopped. Our method has three main strengths: i) inter-farms synergies are accounted for in the LCIA, which is essential to promote circularity; ii) thanks to the innovative and multilevel approach, the origins of environ- mental hotspots can be investigated and later mitigated by future policies; iii) ASD helps to design sustainable scenarios by identifying current trends and regional thresholds (resources, pollution and workforce) that cannot be overcome.

4: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Irrigated agriculture is a good way to increase productivity per vol- ume of water consumed: it provides 40% of food and feed, while only accounting for 18% of the cultivated areas worldwide (Jamin et al., 2011). Nevertheless, irrigation is responsible for 70% of global fresh- water withdrawals (Siebert et al., 2010) and has many indirect environ- mental impacts due to input-intensification worldwide (Foley et al., 2011; Madramootoo and Fyles, 2010). Indeed, irrigated agriculture sus- tainability is a major issue regarding water depletion and pollution due to additional agricultural inputs, as mentioned in the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment (2005). Tools are therefore urgently needed for the exhaus- tive assessment of the eco-efficiency of irrigated agriculture, i.e. its "ability to fulfil a production while minimizing its total impacts on the environment" (Basset-Mens et al., 2009). Kulak et al. (2015) showed that innovative processes leading to more eco-efficient cropping systems were hampered by a lack of knowledge on their environmental impacts.

The environmental impacts of irrigated systems have often been studied in a simple way, i.e. either by focusing on a reduced number of impact categories and/or on a single level or compartment (soil, water, crop, air). Water depletion is often the main impact studied (Llamas, 2005; Lopez-Gunn and Ramón Llamas, 2008; Maneta et al., 2009), but qualitative water degradation originating from irrigation has also been widely investigated, be it at the watershed or plot level (Merchán et al., 2020; Schaible and Aillery, 2012), focusing notably on nitrate field emissions (Pampolino et al., 2007). Later on, based on the energy-water nexus concept (Hussey and Pittock, 2012), the impacts of irrigation on energy consumption (Jackson et al., 2011) and hence on greenhouse gases (Abalos et al., 2014; Maestre-Valero et al., 2018) were investi- gated. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) has been increasingly employed to study impacts of irrigation processes (Grant and Beer, 2008, Pradeleix et al., 2014, McCarthy et al., 2020, Kalboussi et al., 2022). LCA is a framework for quantifying a wide range of potential environmental impacts over the whole life cycle of a product or service, from "cradle to grave", per unit of "function" delivered (e.g., one kg of harvested melon). It informs on any environmental burden shifting, either from one stage of the life cycle to another, or from one impact category to another; water depletion being only one of these categories.

Until recently, research has focused on the plot or the farm level, ignoring potential trade-offs between impacts occurring in the whole farming region but this last level is now prone to active research (Balkau et al., 2018, Bezama et al., 2021). Decision makers need regional LCA to identify environmental hotspots, which should be prioritized to mitigate impacts. Transposing LCA from a linear supply chain to farming activ- ities at a regional scale requires significant modifications among which using a multilevel approach and identifying local data (Verones et al., 2020). This is accomplished by coupling LCA with other approaches to model complex systems holistically, such as the above-mentioned nexus concept (Yang and Campbell, 2017), the territorial LCA (Rogy et al., 2022) or the Agrarian System Diagnosis (ASD) (Pradeleix et al., 2022).

In this paper we implement this last innovative method, which cou- ples ASD and LCA, in the semi-arid irrigated plain of Kairouan, central Tunisia. This case study, as well as the general methodology, were introduced in a previous paper (Pradeleix et al., 2022) which describes

the data collection and completion processes that were proposed to build the Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) as accurately as possible. ASD enabled: the stratification of the farm population, the building of a farm typology, and the characterization of archetypical farming systems and cropping/live- stock systems, which offers two main advantages as shown in Pradeleix et al. (2022). First, it improves the relevance and precision of LCI by facilitating data collection and data completion. Second, ASD enables the multilevel modeling of the farming region activities and also related impacts, which is highly relevant to inform regional policy makers. This paper will therefore describe how the framework created in Pradeleix et al. (2022) has been applied to a semi-arid irrigated plain in Tunisia to deliver an environmental assessment of the present and possible future farming activities, related to irrigation. More specifically, we will:

<list>compute and compare the eco-efficiency of: 
different farming and cropping/livestock systems, 
the farming region, either in the current situation or following two trend-based evolutionary scenarios; 
assess farming system (FS) and farming-activity contributions to regional impacts and identify environmental hotspots.</list>

As the objective of such a study is to inform regional policy makers, a specific impact format was designed, in line with Loiseau et al. (2013) that distinguishes impacts occurring "in-site" (inside) or "off- site" (outside), the boundaries of the farming region. Local decision makers pay particular attention to in-site impacts (Dong et al., 2018), especially water depletion in water stressed locations (Pradeleix et al., 2018).

This work will help public stakeholders conceive more sustainable land planning strategies in irrigated regions, by considering environ- mental impacts, and by identifying specific opportunities and con- straints at each level: from plot, to farm, and to the whole region.

In this paper we implement this last innovative method, which cou- ples ASD and LCA, in the semi-arid irrigated plain of Kairouan, central Tunisia. This case study, as well as the general methodology, were introduced in a previous paper (Pradeleix et al., 2022) which describes

the data collection and completion processes that were proposed to build the Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) as accurately as possible. ASD enabled: the stratification of the farm population, the building of a farm typology, and the characterization of archetypical farming systems and cropping/live- stock systems, which offers two main advantages as shown in Pradeleix et al. (2022). First, it improves the relevance and precision of LCI by facilitating data collection and data completion. Second, ASD enables the multilevel modeling of the farming region activities and also related impacts, which is highly relevant to inform regional policy makers. This paper will therefore describe how the framework created in Pradeleix et al. (2022) has been applied to a semi-arid irrigated plain in Tunisia to deliver an environmental assessment of the present and possible future farming activities, related to irrigation. More specifically, we will:

<list>compute and compare the eco-efficiency of: 
different farming and cropping/livestock systems, 
the farming region, either in the current situation or following two trend-based evolutionary scenarios; 
assess farming system (FS) and farming-activity contributions to regional impacts and identify environmental hotspots.</list>

As the objective of such a study is to inform regional policy makers, a specific impact format was designed, in line with Loiseau et al. (2013) that distinguishes impacts occurring "in-site" (inside) or "off- site" (outside), the boundaries of the farming region. Local decision makers pay particular attention to in-site impacts (Dong et al., 2018), especially water depletion in water stressed locations (Pradeleix et al., 2018).

This work will help public stakeholders conceive more sustainable land planning strategies in irrigated regions, by considering environ- mental impacts, and by identifying specific opportunities and con- straints at each level: from plot, to farm, and to the whole region.
9: The case study was conducted on the semi-arid irrigated plain of Kairouan in central Tunisia. The plain is around 30,000 ha in size, of which 13,000 ha is irrigated. Agro pastoralism, based on sheep rearing and rain-fed olive groves and cereals, was dominant before the El Haouareb dam was built in 1989. Afterwards, family farms began to grow vegetables irrigated using collective boreholes or private wells and cropping systems evolved towards vegetable farming and even fruit orchards, as well as input-intensive irrigated olive groves for those who could afford better water access. Today, family farming is being grad- ually replaced by corporate farming, which benefits from a better access to groundwater abstraction and much higher profitability. During the field study, we observed that the groundwater table depth was 35 m and farmers reported its level was steadily decreasing. In 2004, Leduc et al., reported a maximum annual drawdown rate of -1 m. The field study was carried out in 2012, and 2012 prices were used to calculate the gross product generated by each cropping and farming system.
Table 1 lists the main characteristics of the nine farming systems modeled in Pradeleix et al. (2022).

11: <header>2.2. Life cycle assessment: From LCI data to impacts </header>

The global framework of a LCA is presented in Fig. 1. LCA consists of four steps. The first two steps ("goal and scope definition" and "Life Cycle inventory") are at the heart of Pradeleix et al. (2022), the output of which was the LCI, i.e. quantification of flows of resources consumed and emissions released into the air, soil and water over one year of production in each of the nine standard farming system found in Kair- ouan plain, described above. In step 3, the LCI flows are converted into potential environmental impacts, the so-called Life-Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) results. We used ReCiPe 2008 to model impacts both at the "midpoint" level (e.g., 18 impact categories in ReCiPe 2008 (Goedkoop et al., 2012)) and the "endpoint" level (3 aggregated im- pacts, or "damage areas"). These endpoints are "human health", expressed in "disability-adjusted life years" (DALY), "ecosystem health" expressed in "species.yr", and "resource depletion" expressed in dollars, i.e., the amount of money required to restore resource provision. Step 4 is the interpretation phase. Another parameter to be set in LCIA is the time horizon at which the LCIA model is computed; it can be either long, medium, or short term. We chose the intermediate option (named "Hierarchist"), i.e. ReCipe 2008 (1.07, H).

Environmental impacts were modeled using LCA dedicated software, SimaPro, 8.4. (http://www.pre-sustainability.com and ReCiPe Endpoint (H, 1.07). Upstream processes occurring before farm-level operations (production and transport of inputs), also called "back- ground" LCI processes, were modeled from EcoInvent 2.2, the database libraries incorporated in SimaPro. Foreground LCI processes, i.e., pro- cesses involved in agricultural operations at the farm level were modeled using the ASD-based method introduced in Pradeleix et al. (2022). Field emissions were also modeled to account for the diversity of local conditions (soil, climate) and farming practices (Bellon-Maurel et al., 2015). Water pumping processes were modeled based on Prade- leix et al. (2014). For the sake of clarity and in line with previous con- clusions (Pradeleix et al., 2014), the results are displayed at the endpoint level, but extensive midpoint results are provided in Supplementary Information (Figs. S1, S2, S5 and S6).

Particular attention should be paid to two points that we specifically addressed in this LCA. First, as environmental impacts due to water deprivation are not routinely modeled in impact modeling methods, and given that water scarcity is an extremely important feature in this case study, we included this impact by implementing the site-specific method of Pfister et al. (2009). When applied to our case study, only the "resource" category appeared to be affected by water deprivation. Sec- ond, LCA usually aggregates environmental impacts (of the region) regardless of the location where resource consumption and emissions occur. As already pointed out (Loiseau et al., 2013), we argue it would be helpful for decision makers to distinguish between impact flows taking place inside the region ( in-site flows: local resource con- sumption and emissions released locally) and those taking place outside the region off-site flows). It should be noted that even if some of these flows are emitted locally, they can have global impacts, such as greenhouse gases.

14: Table 2 describes two scenarios for the Tunisian farming region and compares these observations with the current situation (baseline sce- nario). Scenarios are based on the trends we observed during our field work, and interviews we conducted when implementing ASD (Pradeleix et al., 2022): we observed a change in the agrarian system, at the farming region scale, characterized by a reduction in the number of family farming systems (accounting for 50% of total farms), as they could not afford to shift from a surface pumping system to submersible pumps or boreholes in order to adapt to the continuous water table drawdown. In the first scenario, 75% of family farms (FS6 and FS7), i.e., 900 farms, are expected to disappear. The land freed up (6400 ha) would

16: Baseline and prospective scenarios of the farming region and their main characteristics.

|Baseline scenario|Scenario 1: agrarian scenario|Scenario 2: hydrological scenario Water table depth (m)|35|35|110 Water withdrawals (thousands m³|65,525|70,129 (+7%)|65,525 Farming system 6|700|196|700 Farming system 7|550|154|550 Farming system 1|2|12|2 Farming system 2|50|315|50 Farming system 3|50|314|50 Total number of farms|2050|1689 (-18%)|2050
lead to 539 new farms in "corporate farming" systems (FS1 to FS3), according to their respective weight. Consequentially, at the farming region level, 18% of farms should disappear.

The second scenario focuses on water table drawdown, which, based on observations of Leduc et al. (2004) is forecasted to reach a depth of 110 m in 2080. For simplification purposes, all the other parameters of this scenario (including the equilibrium among the FS) remains the same as in the baseline (Table 1). Impacts of water pumping are thoroughly presented in (Pradeleix et al., 2018). At a 110 m depth, only boreholes equipped with submersible pumps would be able to operate, and these currently only exist in FS1 and FS2. The supplementary energy required to pump water from 110 m is based on pumping system performances calculated by Pradeleix et al. (2014), i.e. 19,620 J to lift one cubic meter of water one meter.
Scenarios 1 and 2 could have been linked, as the groundwater drawdown leads to a replacement of family farms by corporate ones. However, for clarity sake, they were modeled separately.
2.4. Modeling LCIA outputs of farming activities, from the plot/ herd to the region scale
The "functional unit" of the LCA we conducted (i.e., the unit used to report impacts) is either an area - one ha of total area used (ha) - or a

gross product - one Tunisian Dinar (TD) (Table 1). To model LCIA outputs, we start from the inventory of consumed resource and produced emissions (LCI), which has been computed at the plot/her level and we aggregate them at higher levels. The whole process therefore follows a "diabolo-like" scaling, from the region to the plot during the ASD phase, and then from the plot to the region in the LCA phase, as shown in Fig. 2. The aggregation of LCI values and the computation of LCIA values up to the whole farming region were carried out as follows for each level.

<list>For each crop belonging to a given cropping system, LCI values are computed at the plot level, and then aggregated at the cropping system level, based on LCI outputs from Pradeleix et al. (2022). 
For livestock, LCI values are computed at the herd level (the func- tional unit being a unit of animal produced), which is common practice (De Vries and de Boer, 2010; Eady et al., 2012). LCI of livestock systems are aggregated with those of other farm level- agricultural processes (computed by ha), according to the area used by each livestock system. 
Next, at farm level, the LCI values of each Farming System are computed by aggregating LCI of cropping and from livestock systems according to their weight, and by accounting for flows exchanged within a single farm to avoid double counting (notably emissions), for instance of impacts of manure stored and then applied to crops, or of crop residues (e.g., of cereals) grazed by livestock. 
Finally, the regional LCI aggregates the farm-level LCI outputs ac- cording to each FS's share in the region, and by accounting for flows exchanged between FS to avoid double counting at regional scale.</list>

4: <header>3.2. Comparison of the environmental impacts of the nine farming systems </header>

The environmental endpoint impacts of the nine existing FS are computed with regard to two different functional units: either one hectare of area used (used for agricultural production, including ran- geland area for FS6 to FS9) in Fig. 4a, or one Tunisian Dinar (TD) of gross product generated, as shown in Fig. 4b. In each of the three endpoint impact categories (human health, ecosystems, and resources), the FS with the most impact is used as a reference and attributed the maximum value (100%). Midpoint impacts are given in Supplementary Informa- tion (Fig. S1 and S2).

Fig. 4a (area-based functional unit) shows similar FS ranking, regardless of the endpoint impact category. FS5 is the worst farming system, because it is the most input-intensive (fertilizers, pesticides). However, FS2 reaches 60% to 80% of the environmental impacts of FS5: this more recent FS has a large share of high plant-density Spanish-type olive groves" ("O /Inp+ (FS2)"), which represents 35% of its cropping plan. Conversely, livestock-based farming systems FS8 and FS9 have the least impact, as they use large areas of rangelands. The performances of the other farming systems lie somewhere between the two, except FS7, which is less impacting and only reaches 28% to 35% of the maximum impact value. Indeed, rangeland represents 33% of its total area used and cropped area is made up of 25% fallow and 18% of low-input cropping systems (18% of cereals and pulses).

Fig. 4b (gross product-based functional unit) also shows similar FS ranking, regardless of the endpoint impact, but the ranking is completely different from that of Fig. 4a: livestock-based farming systems (FS8 and FS9) are the worst due to their far lower profitability per unit of area used (Table 1). The impact of FS5 regarding resources increases slightly, due to the high level of inputs used (including groundwater, which is part of the endpoint impact "resources"). FS1, FS3, FS4 and FS6 lie in between, with regard to both intermediate profitability and impact per unit of area. Whereas FS5 is the most harmful when the functional unit is based on area, it is only fourth regarding market-based FU, as it delivers a huge amount of gross product per area unit.
The reversal of the ranking according to the choice of the functional

unit is in line with other agricultural LCAs (Halberg et al., 2005; Salou et al., 2017). Indeed, in our case study, FS generate highly contrasting values of gross product per hectare of area used, varying up to a factor of 50 between high-input vegetable farms and landless shepherds (respectively, approximately 16,000 TD/ha for FS5 and 300 to 400 TD/ ha for FS8 and FS9; see Table 1).

Recipe 1.07, (H) and Pfister et al. (2009). In each impact category (human health, ecosystems, and resources), the FS with the most impact is used as a reference and attributed the maximum value (100%).

8: <header>3.3. Contribution of farming systems to regional impacts </header>

Fig. 5 illustrates the regional share of each FS with respect, first, to the three environmental impact categories, showing impacts generated by the whole farming region over a period of one year, and second, to the two functional units i.e. the total area used and total gross product generated (Table 1). Material exchanges within and between FS (described in Fig. 3 of Pradeleix et al., 2022) have been taken into ac- count (Fig. 2). It appears that the contribution of each FS to regional impacts is the same, whatever the functional unit. The FS having the biggest impact is FS6 as it is by far the most common in the region (around 45%) and has a medium gross revenue/ha (6933 TD/ha for FS6 whereas the mean over the 9 FS is 7200 TD/ha).

When a FS is significantly harmful per functional unit delivered, but only delivers a small amount of functional units at the regional level, its regional-scale impact can be low. For example, the family business farming system FS5 (intensive vegetable production) is the most harmful per hectare, but occupies such a small area at the regional scale (3%) that it is only responsible for 4.1% to 4.7 of regional impacts. Conversely, the impacts of livestock-based FS (FS8 and FS9) barely reach 2% of regional impacts, despite representing almost 17% of total area used for agricultural purposes, including rangeland grazing.

10: <header>3.4. Contribution of farming processes to regional impacts </header>

The main categories of farming processes responsible for the envi- ronmental impacts of the farming region are shown in Fig. 6. Impacts on "human health" are dominated by fertilizer field emissions (26%), fer- tilizer production (18%), energy required to pump water (14%, be it diesel or electricity) and transportation of farmyard manure by road from outside the farming region (around 13%). Impacts on "ecosystems" are dominated by fertilizer production (21%), energy for pumping (20%), road transportation of farmyard manure (15%), diesel emissions (11%), and field fertilizer emissions (9%).

Regional LCIA impacts can be further analyzed, according to the locations where pollution occurs, either in-site -or in situ-, i.e., inside the boundaries of the farming region (e.g., water depletion) or off-site or ex situ - (e.g., manufacture and transport of inputs). This is shown in

Fig. 5. Regional-scale contribution of each farming system to (from left to right): LCIA endpoint impacts (human health, ecosystems, and resources), total area used, and the total gross product generated. ReCiPe 1.07H, and Pfister et al. (2009) for water resource depletion assessment.
Fig. 6. Contribution of farming processes to regional impacts at endpoint level (ReCiPe 1,07, Pfister et al., 2009).

Supplementary Information (Fig. S5). As usual, most impacts are due to flows taking place off-site (Wood et al., 2006), except for impacts on resources. Respectively, 60% and 73% of impacts on human health and ecosystems are due to upstream flows related to the manufacture of inputs. Regarding impacts on resources, 85% are due to water depletion. This high level of impact is explained by both groundwater overdraft and by the fact that the region is subject to water stress (Pfister et al., 2009).
3.5. Identification of environmental hotspots in a multilevel analysis from the farming region to the process level

The environmental impacts of farming activities can be identified at different levels of the case study using a nested downscaling approach (i. e., region, farming system, cropping system, crop, farming processes). Considering impacts on human health at the regional scale, the mixed "family farming" system FS6 is the biggest contributor to regional environmental impacts as it occupies the largest share (45% of the total, see Fig. S6 in Supplementary Information).

Focusing on FS6 at the farm level, the input-intensive cropping sys- tem (OVC/Wat + (FS6)) is the most harmful (22%), even though it only accounts for 10% of the FS6 cropping plan. This cropping system is made up of olive groves intercropped in a 3-year crop rotation: 1) melon intercropped with pepper, 2) then a cereal, 3) then tomato intercropped with pepper. At the plot level, around 35% of the impacts of this crop- ping system are attributable to the input-intensive "melon" crop, and more particularly to "manufacture and transport of fertilizer", including farmyard manure. Whereas fertilizer production usually has the greatest impacts at the plot level (Ozkan et al., 2004; Pergola et al., 2013), in this case study, it is outweighed by impacts linked to the road transport of manure.

14: <header>3.6. Assessing alternative evolution scenarios for the farming region </header>
Fig. 7 illustrates the regional impacts of two alternative scenarios versus the current baseline situation, regarding the "area used" func- tional unit.

The agrarian scenario (middle column) - i.e., shifting from family farming (FS6 and FS7) to corporate agriculture holdings (FS1 to FS3) - leads to a roughly 10% increase in impacts on human health and re- sources, which is in line with the impact ranking for these FS. Indeed, corporate farms have greater impacts per area used than family farming systems (Fig. 4a).

In the hydrological scenario (right-wing column), the water table level drops from -35 m to -110 m, which results in additional water- pumping energy requirements, and a significant increase in all im- pacts: over 20% for human health and ecosystems, and up to 40% for resources. The environmental impact is therefore much higher than in the agrarian scenario.
15: The development of this new methodology coupling ASD and LCA has led us to identify and tackle methodological challenges that will be introduced and discussed in 4.1. Its application to the Tunisian plain has led to the identification of the main environmental hotspots, which will be described in 4.2. To conclude, the various strengths of this method are summarized in 4.3.
4.1. The methodological challenges tackled by the ASD-based LCA

Applying LCA to a real farming system raises major methodological questions regarding functional units and system boundaries (LCA, step 1, "goal and scope" definition). The case-study perfectly illustrates these challenges.
% of impacts in proportion to the baseline

Fig. 7. Comparison of the baseline regional scenario 0 (in white) with alternative scenarios involving changes in the agrarian system (scenario 1, light grey) and water table drawdown from 35 m to 110 m (scenario 2, dark grey). The area used for agriculture is the functional unit. ReCiPe 1,07, Pfister et al. (2009)
16: Setting time and space boundaries in LCA applied to agricultural systems is highly complex due to the intertwined nature of farming processes; this is a real issue as it has been long known that these settings have major impacts on LCA results and subsequent decision making (Peacock et al., 2011). In the present case study, boundaries -either of cropping/livestock or of farming systems were particularly difficult to identify and characterize due to: the high diversity of crops, intercrop- ping, mixed crop-livestock systems at various levels (plot, farm and regional), land renting between farming systems, and use of public rangelands to graze animals (in FS8 and FS9, particularly). Moreover, material flows, such as crop residues and farmyard manure, were exchanged within and between different farming systems and crossed the boundaries of the farming region.

Although numerous LCA case studies found in the literature study one crop at a time, with a functional unit based on the mass of product, there are an increasing number of papers devoted to LCA analyses of such complex systems, involving crop rotation (Capitanescu et al., 2017; Brankatschk, 2018; Volanti et al., 2021), mixed crops and intercropping (Goglio et al., 2018), as well as mixed crop-livestock farming (Marton al., 2016). In the present work, material flow dynamics are considered over an entire crop rotation and covers not only several crops and years, but also several farming systems and different individual farms. Various examples of methodological issues encountered are given below.

<list>Intercropping is a very common strategy to tackle water scarcity, but raises the question of "to which crop should environmental impacts due to input flows of water, fertilizers, and pesticides applied be attributed?" For example, in the case of fruit orchards intercropped with melons and peppers, manure impacts were attributed to fruit orchards, because this input was destined for orchards, even if intercropped melons and bell peppers indirectly benefited. 
Another issue is the risk of double counting, as public rangelands might be grazed several times over one year. This is particularly the case for FS8 and FS9, where rangelands constitute most of the "area used". Consequently, the midpoint impact named "agricultural land occupation" and the area-based FU, might have been overestimated, (see Fig. S1, S2, S3 in Supplementary Information). Moreover all impacts computed with regard to the area-based FU might have been underestimated, except for "land occupation". 
The complexity of material exchanges between farming systems is exemplified by FS5 land tenants who grow input-intensive water- melons (highest rates of fertilizers and pesticides recorded in our study) for two years on plots rented from FS7 owners. As there is a lack of water for irrigation, FS7 farmers leave these plots fallow for four to six years before renting them out for two years. This fallow period restores soil fertility and eradicates pests and diseases that flourished during the continuous intensive watermelon cropping of FS5. Faced with the question of "to which FS should the fallow period be attributed?", we chose to attribute it to FS7, because it uses the plots during the fallow period to graze its own livestock. If the fallow period had been included in the crop rotation of FS5, its duration would have been four times longer (two years of cropping and six years of fallow); and its average yearly production would have dropped sharply. Consequently, the environmental impacts gener- ated per monetary unit would have significantly increased in FS5 while remaining steady in FS7. Conversely, the impacts generated per unit of area would have decreased in FS5 and increased in FS7. In this case, the difficulty in setting boundaries stems from the fact that, on the one hand, agronomic sustainability is based on crop rotation involving two FS, but on the other hand, environmental impacts are modeled separately for each FS.</list>

19: In the case study, we define two functional units, respectively area and product-based FU, as recommended for agricultural products (De

Vries and de Boer, 2010; Nemecek et al., 2011; Salou et al., 2017): total area used (including area used to produce feed and to graze animals, notably rangelands), and the resulting gross product as a market proxy. LCIA outputs show that most eco-efficient farming systems are strikingly dissimilar, depending on the functional unit chosen, which is in line with the conclusions drawn by other authors (Halberg et al., 2005; Martínez- Blanco et al., 2010; Van der Werf et al., 2007).

Our case study includes livestock systems, for which LCA outputs are usually expressed per animal (De Vries and de Boer, 2010). However, to reflect the complexity of livestock systems, we have chosen to use the area-based FU, and more specifically "area used" which includes different components: livestock-dedicated crop plots, crop residues and cereal stubble, and public rangelands. Crop residue areas are weighted according to allocation rules established for these multi-output cropping systems (Pradeleix et al., 2022). Since rangeland grass productivity is low, large areas are required to feed animals. Consequently, the envi- ronmental impacts per unit of area used in livestock-based FS8 and FS9, which mainly rely on rangelands, are much lower than those of the other FS. However, this should be interpreted with caution (see above "4.1.1 Boundaries").

It should also be noted that many alternative functional units could be used for a farming region, or diversified cropping/farming systems, to take into account the diverse functions they deliver. Indeed, functional units are becoming increasingly complex to define when addressing complex agroecological systems as the latter are highly mixed, diversi- fied and multifunctional (Van der Werf et al., 2020). For instance, Vol- anti et al. (2021) proposed to use a Cereal Unit, that considers both crop characteristics (amount of fibre, protein, carbohydrates, etc.) and the energy content that can be metabolized for food; Marton et al. (2016) built a "basket" FU which comprised milk, live animals sold for meat production, and crops. Because of the size of the farming region and its great diversity, it was too difficult to build a "basket" FU and we have stuck to a more traditional economic FU, i.e., gross product. Gross product has the advantage of being relatively easy to compute, but it does not provide any information about the profitability of agriculture because production costs are neglected. As recommended in the ASD by Cochet and Devienne (2006), when defining FU, it would be more appropriate to take incomes (Nemecek and Erzinger, 2005), or "added value" i.e., the wealth created by deducting the cost of inputs from the retail price.

Indeed, in scenario 2 (Fig. 7), the drop in the water table increases pumping energy requirements and associated costs, thus reducing the "added value" and increasing environmental impacts (from 25% to 40%).

Another relevant functional unit for agricultural activities would be the number of jobs provided, which is a performance index public stakeholders are particularly sensitive to. Agriculture still provides 56% of jobs in the least developed countries (United Nations Committee for Development Policy, 2019). With such a functional unit, scenario 1 (Fig. 7), which entails a shift from a majority of "family farming" to "corporate agriculture" FS, would drastically reduce the total number of jobs provided (FU amount), and increase impacts per FU, to a degree far beyond the 10% of additional environmental impacts already modeled. Indeed, we calculated that around 18% of farms would disappear (360 out of a total of 2050), all of which were family farms. This would be very impactful as family farming is a recognized factor of economic and social stability in territories (Lowder et al., 2014). Only a small pro- portion of people coming from former family farms would be hired by corporate farms, as the number of workers by cropped area is lower for corporate farms than for family farm, as observed during our field work.

21: In the case study, we define two functional units, respectively area and product-based FU, as recommended for agricultural products (De

Vries and de Boer, 2010; Nemecek et al., 2011; Salou et al., 2017): total area used (including area used to produce feed and to graze animals, notably rangelands), and the resulting gross product as a market proxy. LCIA outputs show that most eco-efficient farming systems are strikingly dissimilar, depending on the functional unit chosen, which is in line with the conclusions drawn by other authors (Halberg et al., 2005; Martínez- Blanco et al., 2010; Van der Werf et al., 2007).

Our case study includes livestock systems, for which LCA outputs are usually expressed per animal (De Vries and de Boer, 2010). However, to reflect the complexity of livestock systems, we have chosen to use the area-based FU, and more specifically "area used" which includes different components: livestock-dedicated crop plots, crop residues and cereal stubble, and public rangelands. Crop residue areas are weighted according to allocation rules established for these multi-output cropping systems (Pradeleix et al., 2022). Since rangeland grass productivity is low, large areas are required to feed animals. Consequently, the envi- ronmental impacts per unit of area used in livestock-based FS8 and FS9, which mainly rely on rangelands, are much lower than those of the other FS. However, this should be interpreted with caution (see above "4.1.1 Boundaries").

It should also be noted that many alternative functional units could be used for a farming region, or diversified cropping/farming systems, to take into account the diverse functions they deliver. Indeed, functional units are becoming increasingly complex to define when addressing complex agroecological systems as the latter are highly mixed, diversi- fied and multifunctional (Van der Werf et al., 2020). For instance, Vol- anti et al. (2021) proposed to use a Cereal Unit, that considers both crop characteristics (amount of fibre, protein, carbohydrates, etc.) and the energy content that can be metabolized for food; Marton et al. (2016) built a "basket" FU which comprised milk, live animals sold for meat production, and crops. Because of the size of the farming region and its great diversity, it was too difficult to build a "basket" FU and we have stuck to a more traditional economic FU, i.e., gross product. Gross product has the advantage of being relatively easy to compute, but it does not provide any information about the profitability of agriculture because production costs are neglected. As recommended in the ASD by Cochet and Devienne (2006), when defining FU, it would be more appropriate to take incomes (Nemecek and Erzinger, 2005), or "added value" i.e., the wealth created by deducting the cost of inputs from the retail price.

Indeed, in scenario 2 (Fig. 7), the drop in the water table increases pumping energy requirements and associated costs, thus reducing the "added value" and increasing environmental impacts (from 25% to 40%).

Another relevant functional unit for agricultural activities would be the number of jobs provided, which is a performance index public stakeholders are particularly sensitive to. Agriculture still provides 56% of jobs in the least developed countries (United Nations Committee for Development Policy, 2019). With such a functional unit, scenario 1 (Fig. 7), which entails a shift from a majority of "family farming" to "corporate agriculture" FS, would drastically reduce the total number of jobs provided (FU amount), and increase impacts per FU, to a degree far beyond the 10% of additional environmental impacts already modeled. Indeed, we calculated that around 18% of farms would disappear (360 out of a total of 2050), all of which were family farms. This would be very impactful as family farming is a recognized factor of economic and social stability in territories (Lowder et al., 2014). Only a small pro- portion of people coming from former family farms would be hired by corporate farms, as the number of workers by cropped area is lower for corporate farms than for family farm, as observed during our field work.

24: <header>4.1.3. Methodological recommendations for LCA analysis of complex farming regions </header>
To improve the LCA of complex farming regions, we would recom- mend the following with respect to functional units and system boundaries:

<list>Diverse functions are provided by a farming region; in line with Sala et al. (2017) and Salou et al. (2017) both area-based and product- based functional units should, at minimum, be used. We would also recommend including, if possible, a social-based functional unit, e.g., the provision of jobs. More complex functional units, which may be very relevant when applied to one FS (Capitanescu et al., 2017; Brankatschk, 2018; Volanti et al., 2021), might be difficult to build for a farming region due to the high diversity of production and services. 
Monetary values are useful as FUs, as several agricultural commod- ities can be aggregated, but the monetary values of agricultural products fluctuate significantly and LCIA outputs, accordingly. Such FU must therefore be handled with great care. Furthermore, added value as a FU is far preferable to gross product because it gives more information on the profitability of strategic choices made by farmers regarding annual crops; 
LCIA outputs are highly sensitive to the modeling choices made on time and space boundaries. Consequently, different options regarding boundaries should be explored from a holistic viewpoint to take into account exchanges (e.g. of organic materials) at different levels.</list>
4.2. Highlighting the main environmental hotspots of the Tunisian irrigated plain
Our analysis has identified two main hotspots that will be further detailed here, i.e., linked to water (groundwater depletion), and to fertilization.

4.2. Highlighting the main environmental hotspots of the Tunisian irrigated plain
Our analysis has identified two main hotspots that will be further detailed here, i.e., linked to water (groundwater depletion), and to fertilization.
26: Water is part of a global cycle, and is renewable, yet its local avail- ability depends on the water stress index (Kounina et al., 2012). We adapted the impact modeling method of ReCiPe Endpoint H (1.07) to include Pfister et al. (2009) in the resource impact category, which usually only includes fossil and mineral depletion. As expected, groundwater depletion accounted for 85% of total endpoint impacts on the "resources" impact category. This makes water depletion the main issue for such a semi-arid irrigated region, which is in line with other studies focused on water depletion at water body or river basin scales (Llamas, 2005; Lopez-Gunn and Ramón Llamas, 2008; Maneta et al., 2009).

We evaluated the water consumption of each farming system responsible for intensive water pumping, and the trade-offs, illustrated by the gross product per pumped m3. For instance, FS5 consumes 11,000 /ha, earning 1.44TD/m³, while FS6 consumes 54% less (5000 ha), but earns only 3.5% less (1.39 TD/m3. In addition, in the hydro- logical scenario (2), the water table drawdown would increase the total impact on resources by more than 40% and would contribute to the loss of 75% of family farms, i.e. leading to a net regional balance of -18% of farms. There would be even more impacts if the water pumping rate continues to increase, which suggests that public policies should focus on better management of groundwater withdrawals. Indeed, ground- water drawdown leads to a reduction in the number of family farms in the region, and their concomitant replacement by corporate farms, which further worsens water overuse, due to their far greater water pumping capacity (deep boreholes VS surface pumps). Consequently, more energy costs and environmental impacts and less profitability per volume of groundwater withdrawn are expected, not to mention the social impact of losing agricultural jobs and of the rural exodus to large cities.

28: A traditional significant hotspot in agriculture is the manufacture of fertilizer. We recommend investigating to what extent mineral fertilizers could be replaced by locally produced organic fertilizers, in a way that takes both agronomic and circular economy rules into account, i.e.,

maintaining acceptable yields and enhancing local interconnection flows. When attempting to develop more sustainable farming regions, the implementation of Integrated Crop-Livestock Systems (ICLS), at farm and regional levels, would seem to offer the most viable option (Herrero et al., 2010; Martin et al., 2016).

However, we noted another very impactful process, which was the transport of manure by road, an issue already mentioned in other LCA studies on organic farming (Viana et al., 2022). In our case, this quite unexpected result is explained by the recent segregation of crop and livestock activities at the farm level, and the reliance on "imported" organic matter to maintain soil fertility and productivity, particularly in fruit orchards (up to 10 tons of manure applied per year). Indeed, 99% of farmyard manure comes from outside the irrigated plain, from places where agro-pastoralism is still the rule, and its transport is poorly organized: usually, individual pick-up vehicles are used with a maximum load capacity of 1.2 t for the 100 km round trip. Moreover, we suspect that environmental impacts might have been underestimated, as vehicle parameters were taken from the LCI database (EcoInvent 2.2) and reflect western transport conditions, with significantly more eco- friendly (truck and road) conditions than those actually observed dur- ing our field work in the Tunisian plain. From our surveys, we presume that opportunities exist to rationalize the collection and transport of farmyard manure at the regional scale, and to increase the use of appropriate storage conditions to mitigate N losses as well as to improve fertilizer properties.
4.3. Main benefits of ASD - LCA coupling for multilevel environmental impact assessment

Applying LCA to agricultural regions poses many challenges that have been overcome thanks to the original method we developed; those related to the "inventory" step were addressed in the Pradeleix et al. (2022) publication, and those concerning the LCIA have been addressed in this article. Here are the main benefits we have identified.

4.3. Main benefits of ASD - LCA coupling for multilevel environmental impact assessment

Applying LCA to agricultural regions poses many challenges that have been overcome thanks to the original method we developed; those related to the "inventory" step were addressed in the Pradeleix et al. (2022) publication, and those concerning the LCIA have been addressed in this article. Here are the main benefits we have identified.
31: First, the regional scale analysis proved to be very powerful to identify balances and synergies between different farming systems. Connections of flows should be accounted for, especially in case of heterogeneity, as underlined by Yang and Heijungs (2017) and data aggregation should be handled carefully at this level (Balkau et al., 2018). Our study shows that farming systems FS8 and FS9, based on livestock-mostly fed with crop residues and rangeland grazing- generate significant impacts by monetary FU (gross product). However, even if these livestock-based FS perform poorly with respect to this monetary FU, they are essential as they play many important roles in regional eco- efficiency. They deliver organic fertilizers, exploit residual products overlooked by other FS, use land unsuited for crop production, and grazing regulates vegetation overgrowth on rangelands, thereby pre- venting wildfires (Franzluebbers and Martin, 2022; Oosting et al., 2022; Colantoni et al., 2020). Without local livestock, the manure required for crops and crop residues produced would have to be respectively bought and sold elsewhere and transported by road on larger distances, which would worsen environmental impacts caused by transport. More, in this water-stressed context, cropping and livestock activities do not compete for water since livestock is mostly fed on rangelands and consume relatively small amounts of water compared to the amounts used by irrigation. Both are not only complementary but may also be synergetic: limited irrigation and manure application are the best combination for reducing the blue water footprint (Wang et al., 2022).
32: The power of the ASD-based LCA method for identifying and analyzing environmental hotspots of a farming region based on multi- level approach has been illustrated. This is highly valuable since most regional studies focus on a single level, be it the plot level (Sinisterra-

Solís et al., 2023) or farm level (Avadí et al., 2018) which is not suffi- cient to inform policymakers on land planning priorities, notably regarding biodiversity, land use and social issues (Balkau et al., 2018; Bezama et al., 2021). In our method, the origin of environmental hot- spots can be tracked at every level up to the elementary flow (e.g., manure transportation) and new properties emerge when considering the top level, i.e. the region. The multilevel approach of the ASD-based LCA prevents one from focusing too much on cropping systems having huge impacts, but grown on very little areas. This is well illustrated by the intensive olive groves O /Inp + (FS2)>), for which impact at plot level is huge (Fig. 3), but neglectable at regional level (Fig. 5). This means that its development in the region must be controlled. At regional level, high LCIA outputs can be explained either by the huge impact of the FS or by its large share of land at the regional scale. For example FS6 has huge impacts when looking at regional scale but not at farm level. Last, this method also indicates that a very promising crop management practice could not be upscaled because ASD revealed that its develop- ment is hampered by regional limited resources (water, input, work- force) or dependency on other CS or FS.
These trade-offs between levels are only detectable when a multi- level approach is adopted, as done in our study, with the ASD-LCA coupling.

35: Fostering farm transition to agroecology requires integrating local dynamics into research and farming system assessment (Prost et al., 2023). Prospective scenarios were built from ASD outputs, e.g., the trends observed during field visits, and in-depth interviews conducted during ASD fieldwork. The "agrarian scenario" was built on the observed evolution of the farming system share in the region (i.e. family farming FS6 and FS7 tending to be replaced by corporate holdings FS1 to FS3). The "hydrological scenario", relied on the observed drawdown of the water table. Our choice for the agrarian scenario -i.e. to increase the number of corporate farms while their average cropped area was assumed to remain steady- is open to debate since we observed an in- crease in the area cropped by corporate FS, for the sake of profitability and economies of scale. However, ASD also revealed that the regular shortage of seasonal workers for harvesting could hamper the expansion of such corporate FS. Part of this hard-to-find workforce could be recruited among the former farmers of family FS, provided they have not already moved to big cities as is commonly observed. Furthermore, the scenarios were kept very simple, and this for two main reasons. First, scenario design is not at the heart of our study, which is focused on impact assessment. Second, we wanted to have "model scenarios", in order to clearly disassociate the effects due to the change in the FS typology in the region, and the impacts due to the water-table drawdown. It is plausible that the future prospects of this region might lie in a combination of the two scenarios as water table drawdown is expected to continue, which will cause family farming to leave the place. Digging deeper wells is expensive and only corporate agriculture and family business farms can afford boreholes. Because they cannot pay for such large investments, family farmers are forced to sell their land, most often to corporate agriculture and family business farms. With global changes, including decreasing water resources and rising crop evapotranspiration, along with the increase in the demand for more water-demanding commercial crops, water requirements will significantly increase. In this semi-arid farming region, the current agrarian equilibrium built on several flows interconnecting diverse farming systems is therefore threatened by uneven access to water, which is particularly detrimental to family farms. In the future, these methodological limitations (over-simplicity of scenarios) could be overcome, by taking advantage of other information collected during ASD, in order to build more realistic scenarios. Indeed, ASD can be used to determine the maximum thresholds, regarding both available resources (natural resources e.g., land, groundwater-, human-made materials, local workforce) and acceptable environmental

loadings, linked to local impacts. For instance, in our study, FS7 limited its cropping area due to a lack of available water, while some non-family farmers (in FS1 to FS4) limited their cropping area due to lack of sea- sonal workers. Public decision makers should use such thresholds in their policies to maintain total regional impacts within a "safe operating zone" of regional "planetary boundaries" (Rockström et al., 2009). These thresholds could also inform other approaches for sustainable irrigation and farming system design, e.g., in-silico approaches like linear programming optimization (Acosta-Alba et al., 2012) or partici- patory approaches, which greatly increase the chances of the scenarios to be implemented (Dogliotti et al., 2014; Le Bellec et al., 2011; Ryschawy et al., 2022). Therefore, in order to design appropriate public policies, realistic scenarios should include regional limitations and be built in close collaboration with local stakeholders.

Last, to adequately support decision makers, some key performance indicators should be added and both ASD and LCA can help. For instance, it is important to account for the ecosystem services provided and affected by agricultural activities, some of which can be articulated within the LCA framework (Othoniel et al., 2016; Ripoll-Bosch et al., 2013). Another key performance indicator for public deciders is the number of jobs created, and this information was provided by our ASD- based data collection method. In addition to its contribution to the identification of eco-efficient innovations, the results of the ASD modeling approach could be used as an "intermediary object" (Ostrom, 1991), to support a common vision of a given farming region and its dynamics, drivers, and feedback among different stakeholders (Kanter et al., 2018). Qualitative and quantitative interviews are at the heart of the method as long as feedback sessions which are used to refine results.

To stimulate innovation in more sustainable farming system prac- tices, we strongly recommend involving the farmers and other stake- holders in: the choice of the functional units, the building of alternative scenarios, and the interpretation of results using an iterative approach. The adoption of such participatory approaches in the design of eco- efficient innovations would greatly increase their chance of being implemented (Dogliotti et al., 2014; Le Bellec et al., 2011, Ryschawy et al., 2022).
36: Fostering farm transition to agroecology requires integrating local dynamics into research and farming system assessment (Prost et al., 2023). Prospective scenarios were built from ASD outputs, e.g., the trends observed during field visits, and in-depth interviews conducted during ASD fieldwork. The "agrarian scenario" was built on the observed evolution of the farming system share in the region (i.e. family farming FS6 and FS7 tending to be replaced by corporate holdings FS1 to FS3). The "hydrological scenario", relied on the observed drawdown of the water table. Our choice for the agrarian scenario -i.e. to increase the number of corporate farms while their average cropped area was assumed to remain steady- is open to debate since we observed an in- crease in the area cropped by corporate FS, for the sake of profitability and economies of scale. However, ASD also revealed that the regular shortage of seasonal workers for harvesting could hamper the expansion of such corporate FS. Part of this hard-to-find workforce could be recruited among the former farmers of family FS, provided they have not already moved to big cities as is commonly observed. Furthermore, the scenarios were kept very simple, and this for two main reasons. First, scenario design is not at the heart of our study, which is focused on impact assessment. Second, we wanted to have "model scenarios", in order to clearly disassociate the effects due to the change in the FS typology in the region, and the impacts due to the water-table drawdown. It is plausible that the future prospects of this region might lie in a combination of the two scenarios as water table drawdown is expected to continue, which will cause family farming to leave the place. Digging deeper wells is expensive and only corporate agriculture and family business farms can afford boreholes. Because they cannot pay for such large investments, family farmers are forced to sell their land, most often to corporate agriculture and family business farms. With global changes, including decreasing water resources and rising crop evapotranspiration, along with the increase in the demand for more water-demanding commercial crops, water requirements will significantly increase. 39: The aim of this paper was to show how the original method devel- oped in Pradeleix et al. (2022) could be applied to a real-case study to generate multilevel LCIA results, which enabled us to:

<list>quantify the environmental impacts of farming activities by ac- counting for their diversity at regional level; 
compare the potential environmental impacts of farming activities at various levels: plot, cropping/livestock system, farm and the whole farming region 
identify environmental hotspot impacts at every level and provide solutions to mitigate them, while delivering the same number of functional units; 
estimate the potential environmental impacts in two trend-based scenarios in comparison with the baseline one.</list>

Results show that, not surprisingly, groundwater depletion was a main and critical issue in this water-scarce area. Conversely, the huge impacts associated with manure transportation by road were quite un- expected, and amplified by the need to import large amounts of manure from other regions. In fact orchards require tons of manure each year. Reconnecting cropping and livestock breeding offers a major opportu- nity to mitigate this issue: even though breeding offered low economic returns, it provides free and local manure. At the regional level, the main impacts did not originate from the farming systems ranked the most harmful, but rather from the system having the largest land take-up. Consequently, this work paves the way for LCA studies at the regional level to identify more eco-efficient farming systems in a circular
economy, and notably by enhancing crop-livestock reconnection.

Finally, because of its multilevel aspect, we argue that this meth- odology can be useful both to farmers and to public policy designers. At plot and farm level, farmers, aided by advisors, will be able to under- stand environmental hotspots and adapt their practices to reduce them. At the scale of a region managed by local governance, this method can be used to draw up an inventory of potential impacts, either for the current situation or for future development scenarios. More, ASD can be used both to provide additional indicators of interest to local authorities (e.g. number of jobs) and to build realistic scenarios. These is a last, but not least, argument in favor of coupling of ASD and LCA as a tool to support agricultural policy makers and farmers in the shift towards more eco-efficient farming systems.
